title: Auctions versus Negotiations: The Role of the Payment Structure
abstract: We investigate a seller's strategic choice between optimally structured negotiations with fewer bidders and an auction with more competing bidders when payments can have a contingent component, as is common in mergers and acquisitions (M&A), patent licensing, and employee compensation. The key factor favoring negotiations is that it allows the seller to set her preferred payment structure—that is, the revenue-maximizing mix of cash and contingent pay; reserve prices are of secondary importance. Negotiations are more likely to dominate if synergies increase in bidders' productivity types (as with acquirer-target complementarities in M&A). Higher dispersion and magnitude of bidders' private valuations also favor negotiations.

article.introduction:
A fundamental question in financialeconomics concerns how a seller can maximize revenue when buyers are privately informed about their valuations. In many applications, such revenue maximization involves the strategic choice of whether to spend limited resources—like funds, time, and effort—onoptimal negotiationswith a small group of interested bidders or whether to increasecompetitionby attracting additional bidders who then compete in a standard auction mechanism. A seminal result in the theory of optimal cash auctions is that increasing competition generates higher expected revenue than even the most elaborate negotiation tactics (Bulow and Klemperer (1996)). Yet the restriction to cash bids is an important limitation in various corporate finance applications—such as mergers and acquisitions (M&A)—in which bids are often in securities such as equity. In fact, in M&A, negotiations with a limited number of bidders are at least as common as auctions without negatively affecting revenues (Boone and Mulherin (2007)).
This paper develops a theory of auctions versus negotiations that allows for general security payments. We show that a seller often prefers optimal negotiations to auctions involving higher competition if she can negotiate not only for cash but also for contingent payments, such as performance bonuses, equity payments, or royalties. Indeed, our analysis highlights that the key value driver in equilibrium negotiations is the seller's bargaining power over the structure of payments rather than the power to set a reserve price, which has been the focus of prior work. We derive necessary and sufficient conditions for the seller to prefer negotiating for her preferred payment structure over attracting higher competition. These conditions indicate that sellers are more likely to choose negotiations if the asset for sale adds more value (generates higher synergies) at more productive bidder types, in which case sellers prefer contingent payments while bidders prefer cash; bidders' private valuations are more dispersed; and valuations are generally high relative to bidders' outside options. We discuss the implications of these results for M&A, patent licensing, and employee compensation.
In our model, a seller seeks to determine the most profitable way to sell an indivisible asset. Depending on the application, the asset can be a takeover target, patent, or employee's human capital that firms compete for. The sale is plagued by asymmetric information about interested bidders' productivity “types,” which—together with the allocation of the seller's asset—affects the distribution over contractible cash flows that bidders can realize. This setting has two key features. First, the seller does not know how much any given bidder is willing to pay, but only that bidders' private valuations are identically and independently distributed across bidders. Second, the payment structure, that is, how payments depend on realized contractible cash flows, matters for the distribution of surplus since cash flows are correlated with bidders' valuations.
The main choice the seller has to make in this setting is between “depth” —figuring out how best to negotiate with existing bidders—and “breadth” —attracting more bidders.1Formally, negotiating means that the seller designs the optimal mechanism for selling the asset to then$n$bidders who are already interested in acquiring the asset. This includes setting her preferred payment structure and thus determining the revenue-maximizing combination of cash and contingent pay. The alternative for the seller is to attract an additional bidder to increase competition, with then+1$n+1$bidders subsequently competing in a standard efficient mechanism (akin to an English auction) by sequentially submitting bids in the payment structure of their choice until no bidder is willing to improve on the last standing offer. The central difference from prior models studying the choice between optimal negotiations and an auction with higher competition, such as Bulow and Klemperer (1996), is that the structure of bidders' payments is endogenously determined and not restricted to cash.
To isolate the effect of payment structure on the seller's revenue comparison in the simplest possible way, we initially compare the revenue from optimal bilateral negotiations with that from an auction with two bidders. Apart from being practically relevant, the key advantage of this comparison is that the optimal bilateral negotiation mechanism is a simple-to-characterize take-it-or-leave-it offer, which the seller can formulate in her preferred payment structure, such as cash or equity. This optimal offer balances a standard trade-off between efficiency (likelihood of trade) and rent extraction (information rent left to bidder types accepting the offer).
Asufficientcondition for the seller to prefer bilateral negotiations is that she can design the payment structure to perfectly resolve this rent-extraction-efficiency trade-off and extract the bidder's entire valuation. In this case, bilateral negotiations always dominate because under the alternative of an auction with a second bidder, the winning bid corresponds to the second-highest valuation among the bidders. Since the second-highest valuation is either the same as or lower than that of the initial bidder, it follows that negotiations that always extract the initial bidder's full valuation lead to strictly higher expected revenue. Notably, in this comparison of auctions and negotiations, we rely on the fact that when bidders can choose the payment structure in auctions, they compete in cash. The reason is that, unlike contingent payments, the value of which depends on the bidders' private information, cash payments are not affected by information asymmetries. Hence, by submitting a cash bid, bidders can avoid the problem that the seller undervalues their offers.
A key insight that follows is that anecessarycondition for the seller to prefer negotiations (as with full surplus extraction) is that she negotiates for a payment structure that differs from the one that bidders choose in auctions—that is, for a payment structure that includes at least some contingent payments. To see this, note that if both parties choose cash only, negotiating the payment structure has no value, and the only benefit the seller has in negotiations is the power to set a reserve price. However, when payments are in cash, the value of setting a reserve price is bounded above by the value of attracting an additional bidder, as established in Bulow and Klemperer (1996). We now link these necessary and sufficient conditions to fundamentals.
So, when are contingent payments, and hence potentially negotiations, preferable for the seller? We show that this is the case if synergies and, thus, bidders' valuations increase in their productivity types (henceforth, the “complements case”), but not if more productive bidders have lower valuations (the “substitutes case”).2The reason is that when a payment is contingent on performance, more productive bidder types pay in expectation more than less productive ones. Thus, when more productive types also have higher valuations (complements case), negotiating for contingent payments allows the seller to extract more rent from high-valuation bidders without repelling low-valuation bidders. This mitigates the seller's rent-extraction efficiency trade-off. By contrast, contingent payments exacerbate this trade-off in the substitutes case, in which more productive bidders have lower valuations. Then, the seller would negotiate for pure cash payments, which, as argued previously, implies a lower expected revenue in optimal negotiations than in an auction with an extra bidder.3Optimal negotiations can, therefore, dominate higher competition only in the complements case, where valuations are increasing in productivity.
The sufficient condition that we have presented for negotiations to dominate auctions—that the seller can extract the bidder's entire valuation—is always satisfied in the complements case if standard contracting (limited liability or liquidity) constraints for the bidders are slack. The seller can then negotiate for an optimally calibrated mix of cash and contingent payments that extracts full surplus. If extracting full surplus is not feasible due to binding contracting constraints, we derive a necessary and sufficient condition for (efficient) bilateral negotiations to remain the revenue-superior choice. We discuss the main elements of this condition next.
A key factor favoring bilateral negotiations in the complements case is that the distribution of bidders' productivity (and hence their valuations) is more unequal, as measured by a higher Gini coefficient. To build intuition, consider the case in which higher inequality is the result of a mean-preserving spread in bidders' valuations. Such a mean-preserving spread leaves the expected revenue from bilateral negotiations unaffected, but reduces the expected revenue from attracting a second bidder and conducting an auction. The reason for this is that the auction revenue corresponds to the minimum of the two bidders' valuations, which is lower in expectation following a mean-preserving spread.4
In the complements case, negotiations are also more attractive if the type-independent component of bidders' valuations—and thus, all bidders' valuations—are high relative to their outside options. While higher valuations map into higher payments under both auctions and negotiations, the key difference is that in negotiations, the seller can optimally demand higher contingent payments. This allows the seller to tie a bidder's payments in negotiations even closer to his true willingness to pay. Put differently, the seller can extract more rent in negotiations without sacrificing efficiency.
Underscoring the first-order importance of the payment structure, our paper further suggests a clearpecking order, according to which negotiating the payment structure takes precedence for the seller over setting a reserve price. In particular, we show that the benefit of negotiating the payment structure can be sufficient for the seller to prefer negotiations over higher competition, regardless of whether the seller sets a reserve price. By contrast, in the equilibrium of the entire game, the power to set a reserve price in negotiations has value only when combined with bargaining power over the payment structure.
Finally, we show that our main insights extend to the case in which the seller negotiates with multiple bidders. The primary challenge here lies in characterizing the optimal mechanism to play multiple bidders off against each other when payments can be state-contingent. Under several realistic restrictions, we show that an intuitive modification of a standard two-stage mechanism maximizes the seller's expected revenue. In the first stage, the seller lets bidders compete in an English auction to identify the highest valuation bidder and the second-highest valuation among bidders. Based on this information, in the second stage of the mechanism, the seller optimally negotiates only with the highest valuation bidder, where the optimal payment structure of the seller's take-it-or-leave-it offer is determined by the same factors as in bilateral negotiations. Notably, we show that this mechanism can be implemented in a “details-free” manner, allowing the seller to rank different types of first-stage offers, such as cash or equity, without knowing the distribution from which types are drawn. This is useful in practical applications, as it lowers the burden for a seller to learn about bidders.
Application 1: Negotiating Payments in M&A. A key application of our model is to M&A, where negotiations and auctions are equally popular (Boone and Mulherin (2007)), with targets capturing most of the synergy gains under both alternatives (Eckbo, Makaew, and Thorburn (2018), Dessaint, Eckbo, and Golubov (2025)). Our theoretical results shed light on these stylized facts and help explain the choice between these two methods of selling a firm. In particular, we show that negotiations are valuable for targets seeking equity payments—in line with the evidence in Boone and Mulherin (2007) and Aktas, De Bodt, and Roll (2010)—when the acquirer's and target's businesses are complementary, as well as when synergies are likely to be high and more dispersed.
Application 2: Licensing of Intellectual Property. Our model can also shed light on the licensing and sale of intellectual property. The stylized facts in this setting exhibit many similarities to M&A—for example, negotiations and auctions are both common, and negotiating over the structure of payments is key. What is more, acquiring a firm's patents is sometimes the main motive for an acquisition. The predictions above, therefore, also illuminate standard practices in this context.
Application 3: Compensation Negotiations with Prospective Employers. Tight labor markets force firms to not only outbid their competitors in recruiting qualified employees but also accommodate the demands of candidates who choose to negotiate compensation, which can affect both the level and structure of pay. In fact, in the context of executive compensation, Edmans, Gosling, and Jenter (2023) conclude that: “directors view labour market forces, and thus the participation constraint, as more important than […] the incentive constraint” in shaping compensation design. Our model helps explain why the impact of tight labor markets on the structure of compensation differs across occupations, with compensation increases in some industries (e.g., banking) being driven primarily by negotiations for higher performance or equity-based pay (Bell and van Reenen (2014)), while compensation increases in other industries are driven by competing offers that boost base wages and signing bonuses.
article.I. Related Literature:
Our paper's main contribution is to show that optimal negotiations can be more valuable than an auction with more competition when negotiations allow the seller to choose her preferred payment structure. In particular, we show that negotiating payment structure is worth more than setting a reserve price, which is the main negotiating tactic studied by Bulow and Klemperer (1996) and subsequent work. This insight helps explain why Bulow and Klemperer's (1996,2009) prediction that attracting more competition is more valuable than negotiating often fails in practice. Prior work addressing this “puzzle” does not consider the impact of payment structure, but instead argues that sellers prefer negotiations if bidders find it costly to learn their valuation before bidding (Roberts and Sweeting (2013), Gentry and Stroup (2019)), if some bidders are less informed than others (Povel and Singh (2006)), if sellers can identify bidders with higher valuations (Eckbo, Malenko, and Thorburn (2020)), or if preserving secrecy is important (Hansen (2001)). Furthermore, Aktas, De Bodt, and Roll (2010) argue that when negotiations take place under the threat of an auction, bidders could make preemptive jump bids to deter competitors, making negotiations preferable for sellers (see also Fishman (1988) and Dimopoulos and Sacchetto (2014)). Finally, restricting entry and negotiating with fewer bidders might be optimal when there are entry costs since these are ultimately borne by the seller in the form of lower payments (Bernhardt, Liu, and Sogo (2020)).
The basis for our comparison of auctions and negotiations is that the payment structure in negotiations and competition differs. Our analysis of optimal negotiations is driven by a rent-extraction efficiency trade-off similar to that studied by Hansen (1987), Inderst and Vladimirov (2019), and Liu and Bernhardt (2021). The main difference is that our unified setting with type-dependent outside options allows bidders' willingness to pay to increase or decrease in their productivity, which leads to contrasting predictions regarding the payment structure preferred by the seller in negotiations. Notably, the payment structure in negotiations has a qualitatively similar effect on a seller's revenue as in security-bid auctions (DeMarzo, Kremer, and Skrzypacz (2005), Che and Kim (2010), Vladimirov (2015)). However, the underlying economic forces discussed in the auctions literature are different. In auctions, linking payments to the bidders' types affects auction revenue by intensifying competition among bidders. This force (known as the Linkage Principle) is irrelevant to our negotiation results, in which the seller optimally negotiates over the structure of payments with only one (the last remaining) bidder. Another difference is that this literature restricts attention to so-called “ordered sets of securities,” which limits the seller's ability to extract rent.
A key technical challenge that our paper tackles on the normative side is deriving the optimal selling mechanism for negotiations with multiple bidders when payments can be in securities, in which case some of the standard regularity conditions imposed in the optimal mechanism design literature are not satisfied. Given this challenge, prior work has focused on finding corresponding conditions for linear instruments, such as equity, where optimal mechanisms can be identified (Liu (2016), Liu and Bernhardt (2019,2021)). Inspired by our main applications, our approach instead is to show optimality within the class of dominant-strategy incentive compatible and ex post individually rational mechanisms in which only the bidder willing to pay the most for the asset (if any) wins. The appeal of the optimal negotiation mechanism that we obtain is that it is simple, intuitive, robust, and “details-free.”
article.II. Model:
A single seller (she) seeks the most profitable way to sell an indivisible asset, which is in scarce supply. The asset may be physical or intangible, such as a target company in the context of M&A, a patent, or a skilled worker's human capital in the context of tight labor markets (see SectionVIfor more details on potential applications). The demand side consists of a set of potential bidders (they/he) with deep pockets indexed byi=1,…,n$i=1,\ldots,n$.5All parties are risk-neutral, and there is no discounting.
We assume that each bidder's productivity typeθi$\theta _{i}$—and hence his valuationvθi$v\left(\theta _{i}\right)$—is his private information, and the seller and potential competing bidders only know it is drawn independently from distributionF$F$.7Realized cash flows are contractible, and payments can be in general securities. In particular, if bidderi$i$successfully acquires the asset, he pays according to a contractωi=wi,Δwi$\mathbf {\omega }_{i}=\left\lbrace w_{i},\Delta w_{i}\right\rbrace $that stipulates a payment to the seller ofwi$w_{i}$in the low-cash-flow state and ofwi+Δwi$w_{i}+\Delta w_{i}$in the high-cash-flow state. We refer towi$w_{i}$as cash payment, toΔwi$\Delta w_{i}$as contingent payment, and to the specific mix ofwi$w_{i}$andΔwi$\Delta w_{i}$—as captured by the ratio of contingent to overall payments within a contractωi$\omega _{i}$—as the payment structure. Examples of contingent payments include royalty payments, stock options, or performance bonuses. As is standard in financial contracting, we assume thatwi,Δwi≥0$w_{i},\Delta w_{i}\ge 0$, that is, the seller is protected by limited liability, and contracts are monotone in cash flow (Innes (1990)). Next, we describe how payment contracts are determined.
Our main interest is in determining whether the seller benefits more from higher competition, modeled by a larger number of bidders, or negotiations, that is, bargaining power to choose the optimal way to sell her asset to a given number of bidders. To operationalize this comparison, we follow Bulow and Klemperer (1996) and consider the following game. Att=0$t=0$, the seller must decide whether to increasecompetition—in which casen+1$n+1$bidders compete for the asset in a standard auction mechanism described below—or engage innegotiations—in which case the seller has bargaining power to choose the optimal revenue-maximizing mechanism to sell the asset to then$n$bidders that she already faces.8Our key contribution to the literature is that we evaluate this strategic decision allowing for general security payments, with the payment structure endogenously chosen by the seller in negotiations and by bidders in the auction mechanism.
More specifically, we model competition as an ascending-bid (English) auction with continuously increasing price, where, at any price level, bidders decide whether to continue or quit. Crucially, remaining active bidders can choose how they want to structure their payments (e.g., cash or equity), subject to the condition that these payments, evaluated at the seller's (common knowledge) beliefs, must be at least equal to the current price level. The auction ends when there is only one bidder left. This bidder acquires the asset in return for the payment offer he has made at the price at which his last rival has dropped out.9
If, instead of increasing competition by attracting an additional bidder, the seller engages in negotiations with the existing bidders, she designs the optimal selling mechanism, which includes specifying both the level and the structure of payments. To illustrate the benefit to the seller of having bargaining power over the structure of payments in the simplest possible setting, our baseline specification considers bilateral negotiations (n=1$n=1$). Optimal negotiations then amount to the seller making a simple take-it-or-leave-it offer to the buyer, where we consider contract offers of the formw,Δw$\left\lbrace w,\Delta w\right\rbrace $, including menus of such contracts. If the bidder accepts a contract from the menu, he acquires the asset in return for the payment specified in the chosen contract, or else the sale fails. We extend the analysis to optimal negotiations with multiple bidders,n>1$n>1$, in SectionV. There, we derive the revenue-maximizing selling mechanism within the class of dominant-strategy incentive compatible and ex post individually rational mechanisms in which only the bidder with the highest willingness to pay (if any) acquires the asset. The class of mechanisms that we consider can be motivated by standard robustness, fairness, and limited commitment considerations that are relevant in many of our key applications.10
Following negotiations or competition, cash flows from the bidders' projects are realized int=1$t=1$. If the asset is sold, the winning bidder pays the seller according to the agreed-upon contract.
article.III. When Negotiating Dominates Competit:
Our main result is that if the seller can set the structure of payments in negotiations, optimal negotiations can lead to a higher expected revenue than an auction with an additional bidder. To illustrate the main intuition, we start by showing that if setting the payment structure allows the seller to extract the full surplus in bilateral negotiations, her expected revenue is strictly higher than when she attracts two bidders who compete in cash. We consider competition in cash for this comparison because it is the form of competition considered in prior work (e.g., Bulow and Klemperer (1996)) and in equilibrium the bidders will, indeed, choose to compete in cash (SectionIII.B).
Having shown that the seller can extract full surplus by optimally designing the payment structure, we can now show that the resulting revenue from bilateral negotiations is unambiguously higher than that from the alternative of attracting a second bidder, in which case the bidders compete in a standard cash auction. Specifically, the seller's expected revenue from a cash auction is equal to the second-highest valuation among the bidders. Because the second-highest valuation is the same or lower than the initial bidder's valuation, which is fully extracted in negotiations, the seller's expected revenue from increasing competition is lower than in optimal bilateral negotiations (all proofs are in theAppendix).12
Proposition 1. (i)If the seller can extract the full surplus in bilateral negotiations, her expected revenue is strictly higher than when she attracts two bidders who compete in cash. (ii) Extracting full surplus in bilateral negotiations requires the seller to negotiate for her preferred payment structure as captured by the first-best contractωFB=wFB,ΔwFB$\omega ^{FB}=\left\lbrace w^{FB},\Delta w^{FB}\right\rbrace $. In particular, we obtain an explicit characterization of the payment structure that the seller prefers in bilateral negotiations when the first-best contract is not feasible due to binding contracting constraints. Subsequently, we endogenize the payment structure in competition, showing that bidders will indeed choose to compete in cash (SectionIII). Based on these preliminary results, we derive precise conditions under which the seller prefers negotiations over higher competition when full surplus extraction in negotiations is not feasible, allowing for clear comparative statics (SectionIV). We first focus on comparing optimal bilateral negotiations with competition between two bidders, and then extend our analysis to a general number of bidders in SectionV.
article.IV. Endogenizing Payment Structure:
As we illustrate above, having bargaining power over thestructure of paymentscan have substantial value in our setting. The reason is that the structure of payments matters for how the seller and the winning bidder share the surplus from trade since cash flows are correlated with bidders' private valuations. Accordingly, the payment structure that the seller chooses in negotiations and the one that bidders choose in competition might differ. In what follows, we characterize these endogenously optimal payment structures.

While the optimal payment structure in negotiations depends on whether the seller's asset is complementary to the bidder's productivity type, this is not the case under competition in which bidders—and not the seller—pick their preferred payment structure. To isolate the effect due to the shift in bargaining power to set the payment structure from the confounding effects of competition, we initially consider the case of a single buyer who can make a take-it-or-leave-it offer to the seller.
The equilibrium solution to this problem is well understood: The bidder will offer a pure cash payment. Intuitively, the advantage of a cash payment is that its value is independent of the bidder's private informationθ$\theta$since the cash payment is paid in all cash-flow states. In contrast, the value of all contracts that involve a contingent payment depends onθ$\theta$, such that they suffer from misvaluation in equilibrium. In the unique equilibrium, the bidder offers the lowest cash payment that ensures that the seller accepts the contract offer.15That is, the bidder offers a cash payment equal to the seller's outside option. This intuition extends straightforwardly to the case of multiple bidders. The only difference, then, is that the outside option in (12) is replaced by a continuously increasing price that bidders need to match to remain in the competition. As already discussed, in the equilibrium of this game, bidders compete until the price reaches their valuation, implying that the seller's expected revenue is equal to the valuation of the bidder with the second-highest valuation.
Lemma 3.Suppose thatn≥2$n\ge 2$bidders compete in the payment structures of their choice. Then bidders optimally offer pure cash payments (w>0$w>0$,Δw=0$\Delta w=0$), and the bidder with the highest valuation acquires the asset at a cash payment equal to the second-highest valuation among bidders.
article.V. Negotiations versus Competition:
Having derived the equilibrium structure of payments in negotiations and competition, we are now ready to extend Proposition1. In particular, we characterize the conditions under which negotiating over the structure of payments is more valuable for the seller than attracting additional competition when full surplus extraction in negotiations is not feasible.
First, consider the substitutes case (v′θ<0$v^{\prime }\left(\theta \right) <0$). In this case, the seller optimally negotiates for pure cash payments (see Lemma2), which is the same payment structure that bidders choose to compete on when they have the bargaining power to determine the structure of payments (see Lemma3). Hence, the allocation of bargaining power over the structure of payments is irrelevant, and the only relevant bargaining power in negotiations in the case of substitutes is that of setting a reserve price. However, setting a reserve price is worth less to the seller than the benefit of additional competition. In particular, since the endogenously optimal payment structure is always cash, this revenue comparison result follows directly from Bulow and Klemperer (1996), who exogenously restrict payments to be in cash.16
Proposition 2.In the substitutes case (v′(θ)<0$v^{\prime }(\theta)<0$), attracting an additional competing bidder results in higher expected revenue for the seller than the expected revenue from optimal negotiations with one bidder.

The key point of our paper is that the unambiguous optimality of auctions does not extend to the case of complements, that is, to the case in which the seller's asset creates more value at more productive bidders (v′(θ)>0$v^{\prime }(\theta)>0$or, equivalently,βYβN>1$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}>1$). Negotiations are then more valuable for the seller since the optimal payment method the seller chooses in negotiations differs from the payment method bidders would choose under competition. In particular, the seller optimally negotiates for contingent payments, while under competition, bidders will offer only cash. Hence, in the complements case, it is valuable for the seller to have bargaining power to set the payment structure.
Proposition 3.Consider the complements case (v′(θ)>0⇔βYβN>1$ v^{\prime }(\theta)>0\Leftrightarrow \frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}>1$) and assume that the seller does not set a reserve price. The seller is better off negotiating for her preferred payment structure with one bidder compared to increasing competition to two bidders, who then compete in the security of their choice (cash), if such negotiations allow the seller to reduce the bidder's information rent toEϱθ,0<∫01v′θFθ1−F(θ)dθ$E\left[ \varrho \left(\theta,0\right) \right] <\int _{0}^{1}v^{\prime }\left(\theta \right) F\left(\theta \right) \left(1-F(\theta)\right) d\theta$. This is always the case ifβYβN∈1,αYαN$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{ \beta _{N}}\in \left(1,\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}\right]$such that full rent extraction is feasible under negotiations,Eϱθ,0=0$E\left[ \varrho \left(\theta,0\right) \right] =0$. IfβYβN>αYαN$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}>\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}$, optimal efficient negotiations with one bidder dominate competition with two bidders if and only if (20) holds. This condition is satisfied ifβYβN≤αYαN+κ$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}\le \frac{ \alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}+\kappa$for someκ>0$\kappa >0$, and is always satisfied (i.e., holds for anyβYβN>αYαN$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}>\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}$) ifαYαN≥1G$\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}\ge \frac{1}{G}$.
Based on Propositions2and3, we now discuss the key factors favoring negotiations over competition using Figure1as an illustration. As we establish above, a necessary condition for negotiations to be preferable to competition is that valuations are increasing in bidders' productivity type,v′(θ)>0⇔βYβN>1$v^{\prime }(\theta)>0\Leftrightarrow \frac{ \beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}>1$. In particular, only in this (complements) case does the bargaining power to set the payment structure in negotiations have value for the seller in that negotiating for contingent payments instead of cash (which is preferred by the bidder) improves rent extraction. This benefit is sufficiently strong for negotiations to dominate competition if and only if (20) holds. As is intuitive, this is the case as long as full surplus extraction in negotiations is (almost) feasible, that is, for allβYβN∈1,αYαN+κ$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}\in \left(1,\frac{\alpha _{Y} }{\alpha _{N}}+\kappa \right]$withκ>0$\kappa >0$but small (see Figure1). In what follows, we further discuss the economics behind condition (20) by explaining the key determinants of the differenceΠneg(0)−Πcomp$\Pi _{neg}(0)-\Pi _{comp}$for the case of complements.
Condition (20) shows that a more unequal productivity-type distribution, as captured by a higher Gini coefficient,G$G$, favors efficient bilateral negotiations over attracting additional competition. In fact, if the Gini coefficient is sufficiently large,G>αNαY$G>\frac{\alpha _{N}}{ \alpha _{Y}}$, negotiations always dominate competition in the complements case as illustrated in the left panel of Figure1for the case of a uniform distribution. The intuition for why the inequality of the type-distribution matters is simple to illustrate when we compare two such distributions, with one being more dispersed than the other in the sense of a mean-preserving spread. Since expected productivity, and hence the bidder's expected valuation, is unaffected by an mean-preserving spread, the seller's expected revenue from efficient bilateral negotiations,Πneg(0)$\Pi _{neg}(0)$, is the same under both distributions. This can be seen from (15) after plugging in for the bidder's valuation from (1) and rent from (14). However, the seller's expected revenue under competition,Πcomp$\Pi _{comp}$, is lower under the more dispersed distribution. The reason for this is that a more dispersed-type distribution maps into more dispersed valuations, such that the minimum of the two bidders' valuations, which is what the seller receives under competition, is lower.17
From expression (19) we further see that the differenceΠneg(0)−Πcomp$\Pi _{neg}(0)-\Pi _{comp}$is higher, and thus negotiations are more likely to dominate competition if the ratioαYαN$\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}$is higher. This ratio characterizes the type-independent component of the bidder's valuation normalized by the total upside that acquiring the asset can achieve for any typev(0)pY(0)Δx=1−1αY/αN$\frac{v(0)}{p_{Y}(0)\Delta x}=1-\frac{1}{\alpha _{Y}/\alpha _{N}}$. Intuitively, a higher type-independent valuation allows the seller to demand a higher contingent paymentΔw=1−αNαYΔx$\Delta w=\left(1-\frac{ \alpha _{N}}{\alpha _{Y}}\right) \Delta x$that is acceptable to all bidder types. This allows the seller to move closer to the contingent payment needed to extract all rent from higher types,Δwfb=1−βNβYΔx>Δw$\Delta w^{fb}=\left(1-\frac{ \beta _{N}}{\beta _{Y}}\right) \Delta x>\Delta w$, which the seller would choose absent the binding limited liability constraint. As a consequence, the bidder's rent in negotiations decreases inαYαN$\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}$, making negotiations more attractive. In fact, as seen above, negotiations always dominate ifαYαN>1G$\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}>\frac{1}{G}$.
Proposition 4.Suppose thatβYβN>αYαN$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}> \frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}$, such that we are in the case of complements (v′(θ)>0$v^{\prime }(\theta)>0$) but full surplus extraction in negotiations is infeasible. Then the difference in expected revenue under efficient bilateral negotiations and competition with two bidders,Πneg(0)−Πcomp$\Pi _{neg}(0)-\Pi _{comp}$, is higher if: (i) the bidders' productivity type distribution becomes more dispersed in the sense of a mean-preserving spread, or (ii) the type-independent component in the bidders' valuation, as captured byαY/αN$\alpha _{Y}/\alpha _{N}$, is higher.

Finally, the type-dependent component in the bidders' valuation, captured byβY−βN$\beta _{Y}-\beta _{N}$, affects the revenue comparison by interacting with both economic effects underlying Proposition4. On the one hand, by increasing the type-sensitivity of valuationsv′(θ)$ v^{\prime }(\theta)$, a higherβY−βN$\beta _{Y}-\beta _{N}$leads to more spread-out valuations for any given productivity type distribution,F(θ)$ F(\theta)$, with the amplification being stronger for more dispersed distributions. This effect pushes toward an increase ofΠneg(0)−Πcomp$\Pi _{neg}(0)-\Pi _{comp}$(first integral in (19)). On the other hand, higherβY−βN$\beta _{Y}-\beta _{N}$also increases the level of valuations,v(θ)$v(\theta)$, and thus the bidder's rent in negotiationsϱ(θ,0)=v(θ)−w−pYθΔw$\varrho (\theta,0)=v(\theta)-w-p_{Y}\left(\theta \right) \Delta w$(sincew=0$w=0$andΔw=1−αNαYΔx$\Delta w=\left(1- \frac{\alpha _{N}}{\alpha _{Y}}\right) \Delta x$are fixed by the binding limited liability constraint). This “rent effect” is weaker for changes inβY$\beta _{Y}$thanβN$\beta _{N}$since the former also increases the probabilitypYθ$p_{Y}\left(\theta \right)$with which the bidder paysΔw$\Delta w$. In particular, while the “rent effect” unambiguously dominates for changes inβN$\beta _{N}$, it dominates for changes inβY$\beta _{Y}$if and only ifG≤αNαY$G\le \frac{\alpha _{N}}{\alpha _{Y}}$, as illustrated in Figure1.18We discuss other factors that affect the choice between negotiations and competition, such as seller risk aversion, external financing, and common values, in theInternet Appendix.
The revenue comparison for the complements case (v′(θ)>0$v^{\prime }(\theta)>0$) in Proposition3restricts attention to situations in which the seller does not set a reserve price in negotiations and thus sells to all bidder types. Clearly, giving the seller bargaining power over not only the structure of payments but also a reserve price—as captured by an interior cutoffθ∼*$\widetilde{\theta }^{\ast }$—makes negotiations at least weakly more attractive, as illustrated by the dashed lines in Figure1.19In the following, we focus on the question of which type of bargaining power—over payment structure or a reserve price—ranks higher in the seller's pecking order.
To answer this question, suppose the seller had to choose between setting her preferred payment structure or setting a reserve price (as captured by the optimally chosen cutoff typeθ∼*$\widetilde{\theta }^{\ast }$) in negotiations, with the other type of bargaining power going to the bidder. Denote the seller's revenue in negotiations if she only sets the payment structure byΠnegs$\Pi _{neg}^{s}$, if she only sets a reserve price byΠnegr$\Pi _{neg}^{r}$, and if she chooses both the payment structure and a reserve price byΠnegs+r$\Pi _{neg}^{s+r}$. If the seller were to opt for setting a reserve price without negotiating the payment structure, the bidder would choose to pay cash (Lemma3). But when all payments are in cash, the seller benefits more from forgoing negotiations altogether and instead attracting competition from one additional bidder (Bulow and Klemperer (1996)). By contrast, if the seller chooses to negotiate for her preferred payment structure, the analysis from SectionIV.Aapplies, which shows that negotiating the payment structure can be more valuable than attracting additional competition ifv′(θ)>0$v^{\prime }(\theta)>0$even without a reserve price (Proposition3). These results imply the following pecking order.
Proposition 5.In the seller's optimal choice between negotiations and competition, bargaining power to set a reserve price is valuable for the seller only if combined with the bargaining power to set the payment structure, that is,Πnegr$\Pi _{neg}^{r}$is always smaller thanΠcomp$\Pi _{comp}$whileΠnegs+r$\Pi _{neg}^{s+r}$can be larger thanΠcomp$\Pi _{comp}$. Instead, bargaining power to set the payment structure in negotiations can have equilibrium value on its own (absent the power to set a reserve price), that is,Πnegs$\Pi _{neg}^{s}$can be larger thanΠcomp$\Pi _{comp}$.

Proposition5suggests that by exogenously fixing payments to be in cash, in which case optimal bilateral negotiations reduce to setting a reserve price, prior work comparing auctions and negotiations ignores the key driver of the value of optimal negotiations, which is having bargaining power over the structure of payments. In particular, Proposition5shows that if the seller had to choose, she would pick setting the payment structure over setting a reserve price. Compared to setting the payment structure, setting a reserve price is of second-order importance and therefore is not the right yardstick for evaluating the differences between negotiations and auctions. The following result further bolsters this insight by showing that if the seller has the power to set the payment structure under both alternatives, the power to set a reserve price in negotiations has no value in equilibrium.
Lemma 4.Suppose that the seller has bargaining power over the structure of payments in all selling mechanisms. Then there exists a mechanism with one more bidder under which the asset is sold with certainty (no reserve) that results in higher revenue for the seller than the optimal negotiation mechanism under which the seller has the option to sell with probability less than one, such as when setting a reserve price.

Lemma4extends the basic intuition of Bulow and Klemperer (1996) to mechanisms with general security payments. It implies that if the payment structure (e.g., cash, equity, debt, call options) is the same in auctions and negotiations, then bargaining power to set a reserve price is not valuable in equilibrium, as there exists a mechanism with an additional bidder that sells with probability one and yields higher revenue. A caveat worth mentioning is that this mechanism need not allocate the asset to the highest valuation bidder (i.e., it may be inefficient), and characterizing the optimal selling mechanism with general security payments and multiple bidders remains an unsolved problem (see the next section). On the other hand, the proof of Lemma4is quite generic and also applies to settings with common values, seller risk aversion, and seller private information, which we discuss in theInternet Appendix.
article.VI. Optimal Negotiations with Multiple B:
We now generalize our results to the case in which the firm must decide between optimal negotiations withn≥2$n\ge 2$bidders and competition withn+1$ n+1$bidders int=0$t=0$. Beyond showing the robustness of our main predictions regarding when optimal negotiations dominate higher competition, we provide an explicit characterization of the optimal mechanism in negotiations with multiple bidders and general security payments that is robust, simple to implement, and largely “details-free.”
If payments are restricted to cash, the optimal negotiation mechanism is well understood and can be implemented by a simple two-stage mechanism comprised of an English auction followed by a take-it-or-leave-it offer to the last remaining bidder (Myerson (1981)). Imposing several realistic restrictions on the set of admissible mechanisms, we show that a simple modification of this implementation also maximizes the seller's expected revenue when allowing for general state-contingent security payments. In particular, we define a mechanism as admissible if it satisfies the following standard restrictions (see SectionIand, in a related context, Lopomo (2000)): (i) if the asset is acquired, it is acquired by the bidder with the highest valuation, and (ii) the mechanism is dominant-strategy incentive compatible and ex post individually rational.20The following proposition then holds.
Proposition 6.The following two-stage mechanism maximizes the seller's expected revenue within the class of admissible mechanisms. (i) In the first stage, bidders compete in a standard English cash auction with a continuously increasing price, withw̲*$\underline{w}^{\ast }$denoting the lowest price at which only one bidder remains active. (ii) In the second stage, the seller negotiates the structure of payments with the last remaining bidder by making a take-it-or-leave-it offer ofω={w,Δw}$\omega =\lbrace w,\Delta w\rbrace$that is characterized as follows: (ii.a) In the case of complements (βYβN>1$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}>1$), the seller demands the first-best contractωFB$\omega ^{FB}$ifβYβN∈1,αYαN$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}\in \left(1,\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}\right]$, and ifβYβN>αYαN$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{ \beta _{N}}>\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}$, she demands, for any given reserveθ∼$\widetilde{\theta }$,w=0$w=0$andΔw=1−pN(θ∼)pY(θ∼)Δx$\Delta w=\left(1-\frac{p_{N}(\widetilde{\theta })}{p_{Y}(\widetilde{\theta })}\right) \Delta x$with the optimal reserve given byθ∼*=argmaxθ̂∫θ̂1pY(θ)1−pN(θ̂)pY(θ̂)ΔxdF∼θθ≥θ∼(w̲*,0)$\widetilde{\theta }^{\ast }=\arg \max _{\widehat{ \theta }}\int _{\widehat{\theta }}^{1}p_{Y}(\theta)\left(1-\frac{p_{N}(\widehat{\theta })}{p_{Y}(\widehat{\theta })}\right) \Delta xd\widetilde{F} \left(\theta \left|\theta \ge \widetilde{\theta }(\underline{w}^{\ast },0)\right. \right)$. (ii.b) In the case of substitutes (βYβN<1$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{ \beta _{N}}<1$), the seller demands, for any given reserveθ∼$\widetilde{ \theta }$,w=pY(θ∼)−pN(θ∼)Δx$w=\left(p_{Y}(\widetilde{\theta })-p_{N}(\widetilde{\theta })\right) \Delta x$andΔw=0$\Delta w=0$with the optimal reserve given byθ∼*=argmaxθ̂(pY(θ̂)−pN(θ̂))ΔxF∼θθ≤θ∼(w̲*,0)$ \widetilde{\theta }^{\ast }=\arg \max _{\widehat{\theta }}(p_{Y}(\widehat{ \theta })-p_{N}(\widehat{\theta }))\Delta x\widetilde{F}\left(\theta \left|\theta \le \widetilde{\theta }(\underline{w}^{\ast },0)\right. \right)$.

In the first stage, it is a weakly dominant strategy for each bidder to continue increasing his bids until the point at which he would be indifferent between acquiring and not acquiring the asset. At this point, the bidder drops out. Hence, the last remaining bidder is the one with the highest valuation, andw̲*$\underline{w}^{\ast }$corresponds to the valuation of the bidder with the second-highest valuation. In the second stage of the mechanism, the seller negotiates with the highest valuation bidder by following the prescriptions for bilateral negotiations for the complements and substitutes cases from Lemmas1and2, knowing that this bidder's valuation is higher thanw̲*$\underline{w} ^{\ast }$. Thus, this two-stage mechanism is ex post individually rational. Furthermore, it is dominant-strategy incentive compatible, as all bidders who exit cannot profit at the winning payment, and the winning bidder could not have won or ended up with a lower expected payment by shading his bids.
The restriction to cash offers in the first stage of the mechanism is for ease of exposition only. It is convenient for the seller, as it keeps the first-stage details-free because it does not require the seller to know the distribution of types and continuously update her beliefs. However, we should note that the seller can achieve the same expected revenue as under the mechanism described in Proposition6without increasing informational requirements while allowing bidders in the first stage to submit bids in any securities of their choice. The main challenge with such an alternative is finding a simple, details-free way for the seller to rank general offers since the value of any offer withΔw≠0$\Delta w\ne 0$depends on the bidder's unknown type. To overcome this problem, the seller ranks offers based on the answer to the following question:“What would the expected value of that payment be if the bidderwere indifferent between acquiring and not acquiring the asset at that payment?”That is, offerωB=wB,ΔwB$\mathbf {\omega } _{B}=\left\lbrace w_{B},\Delta w_{B}\right\rbrace $is weakly more valuable thanωA=wA,ΔwA$ \mathbf {\omega }_{A}=\left\lbrace w_{A},\Delta w_{A}\right\rbrace $ifπ(θ∼(ωB),ωB)≥π(θ∼(ωA),ωA)$\pi (\widetilde{\theta }(\mathbf {\omega }_{B}),\mathbf {\omega }_{B})\ge \pi (\widetilde{\theta }(\mathbf {\omega }_{A}),\mathbf {\omega }_{A})$, whereθ∼ω$ \widetilde{\theta }\left(\mathbf {\omega }\right)$is defined in (9). This ranking rule depends on neither the distribution from which types are drawn nor on updating beliefs using Bayes rule. Still, it guarantees that a bidder drops out from competing to acquire the asset only after all bidders with lower valuations have dropped out. Furthermore, by following this rule, the seller can elicit the same information as in the first stage of the mechanism in Proposition6: the identity of the highest valuation bidder as well as the second-highest valuation can be inferred from the last-standing offer{w*,Δw*}$\lbrace w^{\ast },\Delta w^{\ast }\rbrace$according tow̲*=w*+pYθ∼w*,Δw*Δw*$\underline{w}^{\ast }=w^{\ast }+p_{Y}\left(\widetilde{\theta }\left(w^{\ast },\Delta w^{\ast }\right) \right) \Delta w^{\ast }$. We formalize this discussion and provide more details in PropositionIA1in theInternet Appendix.21
To summarize, the optimal negotiation mechanism is simple: The seller runs a standard English auction or details-free security auction and must negotiate only with the last remaining bidder. Furthermore, note that optimal negotiations withn$n$bidders can be thought of as adding a final take-it-or-leave-it offer to the outcome under competition withn$n$bidders. Thus, for our main revenue comparison, the key question is whether the additional revenue from the second-stage take-it-or-leave-it offer to one bidder (here, the bidder with the highest valuation amongn$n$bidders) dominates the additional revenue from adding another bidder (here, then+1st$ n+1^{{\rm st}}$bidder) to the English auction. Since this question is similar to the baseline case of SectionIV, it is straightforward that the main results from Propositions2and3extend to the case withn≥2$n\ge 2$.
Proposition 7.Suppose that the seller facesn≥2$ n\ge 2$bidders in negotiations. (i) In the substitutes case (v′(θ)<0$v^{\prime }(\theta)<0$, or equivalentlyβYβN<1$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}<1$), competition withn+1$n+1$bidders dominates optimal negotiations withn$n$bidders. (ii) In the complements case (v′(θ)>0$v^{\prime }(\theta)>0$, or equivalentlyβYβN>1$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}>1$), the seller strictly prefers negotiations ifβYβN∈1,αYαN$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}\in \left(1,\frac{\alpha _{Y} }{\alpha _{N}}\right]$, in which case, she can extract full surplus by negotiating for her preferred payment structure. If full surplus extraction is not feasible,βYβN>αYαN$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}>\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}$, efficient negotiations (i.e.,θ∼=θ∼(w̲*,0)$\widetilde{\theta }=\widetilde{\theta }(\underline{w}^{\ast },0)$in the second-stage of the optimal negotiation mechanism) dominate competition if and only if


Proposition7extends the main qualitative insights from Propositions2and3to the case of multiple bidders. It shows that the seller prefers negotiating for her preferred payment structure over increasing competition only in the case of complements (βYβN>1$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{ \beta _{N}}>1$) if this allows her to sufficiently reduce the winning bidder's information rent. As in SectionIV, this is the case if negotiations allow for full surplus extraction sinceβYβN∈1,αYαN$ \frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}\in \left(1,\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}} \right]$. IfβYβN>αYαN$\frac{\beta _{Y}}{\beta _{N}}>\frac{\alpha _{Y}}{\alpha _{N}}$, so that full-surplus extraction is not feasible, expression (21)—which is the multiple-bidder analogue to (20)—provides a necessary and sufficient condition.22Notably, just as in our core analysis of SectionIV, we show that negotiations can dominate competition even if the seller does not set a reserve price.
We now briefly discuss the case in which the negotiating seller has bargaining power only vis-à-vis a subset of biddersB⊂1,…,n$B\subset \left\lbrace 1,\ldots,n\right\rbrace $, where we assume that the distribution of bargaining power is independent of a bidder's productivity type. While this clearly makes negotiations less valuable relative to competition, the basic insights of Propositions6and7extend. In particular, consider the case in which the seller is able to make a take-it-or-leave-it offer in negotiations only if the winning bidder belongs toi∈B$i\in B$, whereas she cannot negotiate with the winning bidder ifi∉B$i\notin B$. Then the following modification of the mechanism in Proposition6is optimal: While the seller cannot restrict all first-stage bids to cash, she can still enforce the competition mechanism described in SectionI, which is outcome-equivalent since bidders choose to bid in cash in equilibrium (Lemma3). Hence, the only difference arises in the second stage of the mechanism. If, for the last remaining bidder, it holds thati∈B$i\in B$, the seller makes a final take-it-or-leave-it offer as characterized in Proposition6. Otherwise, ifi∉B$i\notin B$, the winning bidder acquires the asset at the price at which his last rival bidder drops out. Clearly, in terms of comparing auctions with negotiations, the negotiation mechanism above becomes more attractive as the number of bidders inB$B$increases.
article.VII. Applications:
Both auctions and negotiations are commonly observed in various (corporate finance) applications, with negotiations typically involving bargaining over the structure of payments. In the following, we discuss how our model can contribute to a better understanding of several such applications and derive novel testable implications. In theInternet Appendix, we consider various extensions of our baseline model and explain how they can shed further light on the applications we discuss below. These extensions include the effect of common values, seller risk aversion, and the impact of cash constraints and external financing.
Framed in the context of M&A, our theory predicts the following cross-sectional variation in the method of payment (see Lemmas1to3).
Implication 1.The payment method in M&A will differ systematically depending on whether a target firm is sold via negotiations or an auction. (i) In an auction, cash payments will be preferred. (ii) In negotiations, cash payments will be prevalent if the acquirer's and target's businesses are substitutes. However, if their businesses are complementary, targets are more likely to negotiate for equity payments.

Implication1sheds light on the evidence that equity payments are more likely in bilateral negotiations, while cash payments are more likely when there are multiple bidders competing in a takeover auction (Boone and Mulherin (2007), Betton, Eckbo, and Thorburn (2008), Aktas, De Bodt, and Roll (2010)). It further helps explain why equity payments are more likely when there is a higher degree of complementarity between targets and acquirers (Eckbo, Makaew, and Thorburn (2018)). Two firms' businesses are more likely to be complementary if their products can be bundled and can reinforce each other's demand, as in the case of a merger between a camera manufacturer and a lens manufacturer, or between a hardware company and a software company (Katz and Shapiro (1994)).23By contrast, firms that sell more standardized goods and compete on price are more likely to be substitutes (Carlton and Perloff (2015)), as in a horizontal merger between two (online) retailers or between two airlines.
In practice, about half of M&A deals are negotiated with a single acquirer. What is notable, however, is that average takeover premia between M&A auctions and negotiations do not differ, and in either case, targets seem to extract most of the estimated synergies (Boone and Mulherin (2007), Aktas, De Bodt, and Roll (2010), Eckbo, Makaew, and Thorburn (2018), Dessaint, Eckbo, and Golubov (2025)). Thus, neither auctions nor negotiations are unambiguously preferred by targets, making it important to understand the strategic choice between the two. This leads to the following predictions:
Implication 2.Targets are more likely to prefer negotiations with fewer (n$n$) potential acquirers over a standard auction with more (n+1$n+1$) potential acquirers if: (i) the target's and acquirer's businesses are complementary (rather than substitutes), (ii) synergies are substantial, or (iii) synergies are more dispersed.

While we are not aware of empirical work on the choice between negotiations and auctions testing for all factors we predict in Implication2, partially suggestive evidence exists. Consistent with prediction (iii), Gentry and Stroup (2019) find that negotiations are preferred in takeover markets in which acquirers know more about targets. To see the link between information about the match of acquirers and the target and the dispersion in acquirers' valuations, note that when acquirers are uninformed about a target, their valuations will be more concentrated around the mean. By contrast, if acquirers have more precise information—for example, a more detailed understanding of the synergies from integrating a target into their respective supply chain, valuations will be more dispersed around the mean (Ganuza and Penalva (2010)).24
We should emphasize that the implications above are derived in the context of independent private values and hence are particularly likely to apply to strategic acquirers for which private, acquirer-specific synergies play a dominant role (Gorbenko and Malenko (2024)). We discuss how common values, which are more relevant in the case of financial acquirers, affect the choice between auctions and negotiations and point to supportive evidence in theInternet Appendix.25
The licensing of intellectual property, patents, and trademarks is another setting in which our insights can apply. This setting is interesting from an (empirical) corporate finance perspective for at least two reasons. First, data containing the financial terms of licensing deals have recently become available (Shah, Vaughan, and Ledley (2023)). Second, while systematic evidence is still scant, accounts by practitioners suggest that there are many parallels to M&A deals. In particular, similar to M&A deals, bilateral negotiations are more common than publicly held auctions. Also similar to M&A, the main argument for the apparent prevalence of bilateral negotiations is that they allow for more in-depth bargaining over deal terms, such as royalty rates (Jarosz et al. (2010)). Royalty rates are typically 5% to 10% of sales (Hegde (2014), Shah, Vaughan, and Ledley (2023)), but negotiations play a key role, as royalties vary widely and can often exceed 20%, as evidenced in the $1 billion deal between Takeda and Orexigen Therapeutics in 2010, in which Orexigen negotiated for royalty rates of 20% to 35%.26
Also similar to M&A deals, anecdotal evidence suggests that cash payments are often preferred in auctions. For instance, Microsoft won an auction in 2012 for more than 800 of AOL's patents for $1.06 billion, paid in cash. Further underscoring the link to M&A, in some cases, the main goal of an M&A deal is to acquire the target's patent portfolio, such as in the auction of Motorola Mobility, which Google acquired in 2012 for $12.5 billion in cash. Implications1and2could shed light on such anecdotal evidence and guide more research on the topic.27
Auctions and negotiations with bargaining over the payment method are also common in several other related applications, including (i) the sale of rights to explore and exploit natural resources (Bhattacharya, Ordin, and Roberts (2022), Covert and Sweeney (2023)), (ii) the sale of broadcasting rights (Klemperer (2002)), (iii) infrastructure concessions (Athias and Nunez (2008)), and (iv) real estate lease transactions (Chow, Hafalir, and Yavas (2015)). While discussing each of these applications in detail is beyond the scope of this paper, the factors affecting the choice between auctions and negotiations highlighted in our baseline model, along with those discussed in theInternet Appendix, should also be relevant in these cases.28
Our paper could also shed light on compensation design and its determinants. In tight labor markets, attracting talented workers often forces firms to deviate from their preferred compensation structure to outbid their competitors or accommodate workers' demands.29This problem has plagued recruitment of both top executives and rank-and-file employees, with recent academic work arguing that directors see competition for talent as being more important for compensation design than providing effort incentives (Edmans, Gosling, and Jenter (2023)). Applied to this context, our key novel predictions can be summarized as follows.
Implication 3. (i)A worker prefers negotiating with fewer (n$n$) interested firms compared to collecting offers from more (n+1$n+1$) firms if her skills complement a prospective employer's business, creating more value at more productive firms. In this case, the worker prefers in-depth negotiations for equity-based or performance pay. This particularly applies when employers' valuations of workers' human capital are likely to be high and more dispersed. (ii) Instead of negotiating, a worker can extract higher compensation by generating competing offers to increase her fixed pay if her skills create more value at lower productivity firms.

Implication3predicts that negotiations, which are common for about 30% to 55% of jobs (Hall and Krueger (2012), Brenzel, Gartner, and Schnabel (2014), Robert Half (2019)), will be relevant for jobs in which workers' skills are complementary to firms' businesses. Empirically, this is more likely the case for high-skilled workers in positions in which teamwork and specialized skills are important. A typical example is when an executive joins a firm with healthy growth prospects, with higher skilled executives creating more value at higher potential firms. Complementarity among team members is also considered paramount in innovative businesses, such as high-growth startups. Consistent with our prediction regarding equilibrium pay structure in negotiations, employees in the financial sector, which also features a high degree of such complementarity (Célérier and Vallée (2019)), have seen pay increases in tight labor markers primarily in the form of performance bonuses and equity-based pay (Bell and Van Reenen (2014), Giannetti and Metzger (2015)). Furthermore, equity-based pay is particularly common in tech, biotech, and business services (Kedia and Rajgopal (2009)), where skill complementarity is also important.
Implication3has starkly different predictions when workers create more value at lower productivity firms. Examples of such workers include a maintenance engineer or an executive with restructuring skills whose skills substitute for the firm's lower quality assets. The case of substitutes is also often associated with lower skilled workers, as their skills are more likely to overlap, or jobs at risk of being automated away (Larrain (2015), Fonseca and van Doornik (2022)). Consistent with Implication3, the anecdotal evidence is that lower skilled workers (whose skills are typically considered substitutable), such as warehouse managers, grocery store employees, or truck drivers, have seen pay increases during hot labor markets in the form of higher hourly wages and sign-on bonuses.30
Overall, our theory can help explain why the importance of negotiations and competition in shaping compensation differs across professions and how this affects equilibrium pay structure. From a corporate finance perspective, understanding these determinants of the structure of compensation is highly relevant since they affect firms' ability not only to attract but also retain employees (Hoffmann and Vladimirov,2025; Terovitis and Vladimirov,2025), firms' funding needs, and the profits left for nonemployee owners.31
article.VIII. Conclusion:
Negotiations over payments are pervasive in various settings of interest in financial economics, ranging from M&A to negotiating employee compensation packages. This popularity of negotiations is difficult to reconcile with the benchmark theory of optimal cash auctions, which has argued that even the most elaborate negotiation tactics are worth little compared to acquiring another bidder and running a standard auction without a reserve price (Bulow and Klemperer (1996)). We contribute to the debate trying to explain this discrepancy between theory and practice by explicitly considering that the structure of payments can be part of the seller's negotiation agenda. In particular, we show that the key value driver in negotiations is not the power to set a reserve price, which is the focus of prior work, but rather the seller's power to negotiate for her preferred mix of cash and contingent payments.
We show that under realistic conditions, the rent-extraction benefit of negotiating for contingent payments, such as equity or performance pay, dominates the increase in revenue brought about by acquiring another bidder. In particular, negotiations are more likely to dominate if the asset for sale creates higher value at more productive bidder types, bidders' valuations are more dispersed, and valuations are high across all bidder types. On the normative side, our paper further contributes to the literature by characterizing an optimal, robust, and “details-free” selling mechanism in which payments can be in contingent claims, and the seller may have bargaining power vis-à-vis some bidders but not others.
We obtain our results in a parsimonious independent private-values setting with universal risk-neutrality. Depending on the specific application, factors such as common values, risk aversion, or financing constraints may also affect the seller's strategic choice between negotiations and competition. While we discuss the qualitative effect of these factors, a full-fledged analysis represents an interesting avenue for further research. Similarly, while ours is a model of one-sided private information, in some applications sellers may know more about the features or quality of the asset for sale than bidders do. Our model lends itself to incorporating such seller private information as well as the decision to share such information with bidders. In particular, the seller may target information provision more closely to individual bidders in negotiations, and the mere choice of negotiations rather than competition may reveal some of her private information. Such extensions may also be interesting for future research.

Since the initial bidders do not participate in the second stage, their strategies in the first stage are unaffected, implying that the seller's expected revenue from the first stage is the same as in the original mechanism.
